children.name: Eutardigrada
children.description: The larger of the two tardigrade classes, lacking the lateral sensory appendages of heterotardigrades. Eutardigrades include the majority of described species and most of the well-studied extremophiles. They inhabit mosses, lichens, leaf litter, soil, and freshwater across every continent including Antarctica.

children.name: Ramazzottius
children.description: Among the most frequently encountered tardigrade genera — Ramazzottius oberhaeuseri is often the first tardigrade a student sees, found reliably in roof gutters, bird baths, and any patch of dried moss. Despite its mundane habitat it is a remarkable cryptobiote, capable of surviving almost complete desiccation by forming a tun state, then rehydrating and walking away after years or decades.

children.name: Ramazzottius varieornatus
children.description: One of the most stress-tolerant tardigrades known; its genome revealed the protein Dsup, which shields DNA from radiation damage and even protects human cells when expressed in them.

children.name: Ramazzottius coronarifer
children.description: A European and Asian moss-dwelling eutardigrade distinguished by the characteristic ring-like ornaments encircling its eggs. It forms cryptobiotic tuns during desiccation and is commonly recovered from rooftop and wall mosses in temperate regions.

children.name: Ramazzottius rudescui
children.description: Described from Romanian and Central European mosses, this species is distinguished from close relatives by subtle differences in its cuticular granulation and egg surface sculpture. It inhabits epilithic lichens and bark mosses, entering anhydrobiosis readily upon desiccation.

children.name: Hypsibius
children.description: A genus containing the principal model organism of tardigrade biology. Hypsibius exemplaris was the first tardigrade to have its full genome sequenced, revealing surprisingly little horizontal gene transfer from bacteria — overturning an earlier claim that tardigrades had acquired 17% of their genome from other organisms. It is easy to culture in the laboratory and has a short generation time, making it ideal for developmental and genetic studies.

children.name: Hypsibius convergens
children.description: A widespread limno-terrestrial tardigrade of mosses and freshwater, long confused with H. dujardini in laboratory cultures until molecular work separated the two.

children.name: Hypsibius scabropygus
children.description: Named for the distinctive rough sculpturing of its posterior cuticle, this freshwater-associated species is found among submerged mosses and aquatic vegetation in ponds and slow-moving streams. It forms tuns under drought stress and tolerates mild freezing.

children.name: Hypsibius pallidus
children.description: A pale, lightly pigmented species inhabiting freshwater mosses and algal mats in lakes and ponds. Its widespread distribution reflects the passive dispersal ability of tardigrades via wind, water, and animal vectors; it is occasionally recovered from moist soil as well as true aquatic habitats.

children.name: Macrobiotus
children.description: The founding genus of tardigrade taxonomy, established in 1834 when Schultze described Macrobiotus hufelandi — one of the first tardigrades ever formally named. Macrobiotus species are characterised by their ornate, sculptured eggs, which are among the most taxonomically informative features in the group. New Macrobiotus species are still being described regularly, often from moss samples collected in city parks.

children.name: Macrobiotus shonaicus
children.description: Described in 2018 from a moss cushion on a Japanese apartment balcony, it can be reared and bred entirely in the lab and lays eggs with distinctive flexible filaments.

children.name: Macrobiotus persimilis
children.description: A member of the Macrobiotus hufelandi complex separated by the fine ornamentation of its eggs, a hallmark feature used to tell apart these otherwise near-identical species.

children.name: Macrobiotus harmsworthi
children.description: One of the most cosmopolitan tardigrades, collected from mosses and lichens across every continent including sub-Antarctic islands. Its nearly global distribution likely reflects excellent passive dispersal and high tolerance of environmental extremes including freeze-thaw cycles.

children.name: Macrobiotus pseudohufelandi
children.description: A member of the hufelandi group separated from the nominate species by differences in egg pore structure and cuticular granulation. It occurs in epiphytic mosses and bark lichens in temperate forests and is capable of prolonged anhydrobiosis.

children.name: Macrobiotus polonicus
children.description: Described from Polish moss samples, this species is characterised by the regular polygonal pattern of its egg surface — a feature only fully resolved by scanning electron microscopy. It is associated with epilithic and epiphytic mosses in central and eastern Europe.

children.name: Macrobiotus furciger
children.description: Named for the forked processes on its egg surface, Macrobiotus furciger inhabits moist mosses, soil films, and leaf litter across warm-temperate and subtropical regions. It tolerates moderate desiccation by forming tuns and can survive short periods of freezing.

children.name: Macrobiotus sandrae
children.description: A recently described European species distinguished by its egg surface ornamentation of conical processes with expanded bases. It is found in roof mosses and epilithic lichens and is named after the collector who first isolated the type material.

children.name: Macrobiotus spectabilis
children.description: A visually striking species whose eggs bear tall, well-separated conical processes, making them easily recognisable under light microscopy. It inhabits cushion mosses on boulders and walls in cool temperate habitats and is moderately tolerant of desiccation.

children.name: Paramacrobiotus
children.description: A genus of medium-sized eutardigrades found in mosses and lichens across the world. Paramacrobiotus richtersi is notable for being among the most radiation-resistant animals known — it can survive doses of ionising radiation that would be lethal to virtually all other multicellular life, likely by rapidly repairing DNA damage and by producing protective proteins during its desiccated tun state.

children.name: Paramacrobiotus experimentalis
children.description: Described in 2021 from cultures, this parthenogenetic species has become a model for studying anhydrobiosis and the molecular biology of surviving complete drying.

children.name: Paramacrobiotus craterlakensis
children.description: Described from moss and lichen samples collected near Crater Lake, Oregon, this species inhabits cool montane habitats in the Pacific Northwest. It is characterised by the polygonal pattern of its egg surface and is one of several Paramacrobiotus species found at high-elevation volcanic landscapes.

children.name: Paramacrobiotus palaui
children.description: An island-endemic eutardigrade collected from mosses and algal mats in the Palau archipelago. It represents part of the undersampled tropical Pacific tardigrade fauna and is distinguished from related species by the proportions of its buccal tube and pharyngeal bulb.

children.name: Paramacrobiotus fairbanksi
children.description: A cold-adapted Paramacrobiotus collected from boreal and subarctic mosses in Alaska and Siberia. It forms robust cryptobiotic tuns that survive deep freezing and has been recovered from permafrost-adjacent habitats, making it a subject of interest for cryobiology research.

children.name: Paramacrobiotus tonollii
children.description: A European mountain species found in lichens and cushion mosses at elevations above 1000 m. Its egg surface bears a fine reticulate pattern diagnostic within the richtersi group, and it has been recovered from alpine fellfields and glacial forelands.

children.name: Mesobiotus
children.description: A genus recently segregated from Macrobiotus on the basis of molecular phylogenetics and egg morphology, Mesobiotus species occupy mosses, soils, and freshwater sediments across temperate and tropical regions. The harmsworthi group within this genus has been subject to ongoing taxonomic revision as integrative methods resolve cryptic diversity.

children.name: Mesobiotus harmsworthi
children.description: The type species of the harmsworthi group and one of the most frequently cited tardigrades in ecological surveys. It inhabits mosses and lichens on rock, soil, and bark in both temperate and tropical zones and enters anhydrobiosis readily.

children.name: Mesobiotus pastoris
children.description: Found predominantly in soil and leaf litter of pastured and cultivated land, Mesobiotus pastoris tolerates mild disturbance and moderate desiccation. It is one of the few tardigrades regularly encountered in agricultural soil surveys, where it preys on bacteria and algae.

children.name: Mesobiotus occultus
children.description: A cryptic species long overlooked within the harmsworthi complex and only resolved through integrative taxonomy combining molecular markers with SEM egg analysis. It inhabits epilithic mosses and can survive several months of complete desiccation as an anhydrobiotic tun.

children.name: Minibiotus
children.description: Small but conspicuous eutardigrades found in mosses and lichens worldwide, Minibiotus species have relatively smooth cuticles and simple claws compared to Macrobiotus. The genus is notable for the occurrence of species with markedly different claw types within the same clade, making claw morphology an unreliable sole guide to identification. Molecular phylogenetics has greatly clarified species boundaries.

children.name: Minibiotus intermedius
children.description: The most frequently recorded Minibiotus species and one of the most cosmopolitan tardigrades; it is found in mosses on walls, roofs, rocks, and tree bark across all inhabited continents. Its tolerance of urban pollution and disturbance makes it a useful bioindicator.

children.name: Minibiotus scrutatus
children.description: Named for the careful examination required to distinguish it from congeners, M. scrutatus is found in epilithic and epiphytic mosses in humid temperate forests. Fine differences in its egg pore size and distribution are the primary diagnostic characters.

children.name: Minibiotus pentannulatus
children.description: Described from South American moss samples, this species is identified by the five annular ridges encircling the upper portion of its egg processes, a distinctive character visible by light microscopy. It inhabits moist cushion mosses in subtropical and tropical habitats.

children.name: Minibiotus furcatus
children.description: Characterised by the bifurcated secondary branches of its claws, this species inhabits mosses on humid rock surfaces and forest floor litter. Its forked claw structure is one of the clearest examples in the genus of how diverse claw morphology can be within Minibiotus.

children.name: Milnesium
children.description: Predatory tardigrades — unusual in the phylum for actively hunting and consuming other tardigrades, rotifers, and nematodes using a pair of stylets that pierce prey and suck out cell contents. Milnesium tardigradum is one of the most studied extremophiles in biology: it can survive temperatures from -272°C to +151°C, pressures up to 6,000 atmospheres, and radiation doses of 570,000 roentgens — over 1,000 times the lethal dose for a human.

children.name: Milnesium asiaticum
children.description: A predatory milnesiid distinguished by its claw configuration, hunting rotifers, nematodes, and smaller tardigrades among Asian mosses and lichens.

children.name: Milnesium berladnicorum
children.description: Described from Romanian lowland mosses near the Berlad river valley, this predatory species hunts rotifers and small nematodes in moist epilithic and epiphytic substrates. It is distinguished from M. tardigradum by differences in primary claw branch ratios.

children.name: Milnesium kogui
children.description: A tropical predatory tardigrade described from Colombian cloud forest mosses, named after the Kogi indigenous people of the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta. It inhabits high-humidity epiphytic bryophytes and is part of the rich South American Milnesium fauna currently being surveyed.

children.name: Milnesium beatae
children.description: A predatory eutardigrade from European and Central Asian moss habitats, distinguished by its claw proportions and pharyngeal apophyses. Like other milnesiids it actively pierces and consumes invertebrate prey and enters cryptobiosis when its habitat desiccates.

children.name: Richtersius
children.description: A small genus named after the Swedish zoologist Claudia Richters. Richtersius coronifer is particularly notable as one of the tardigrades sent aboard the FOTON-M3 spacecraft in 2007 — the first animals ever confirmed to survive the complete vacuum and intense UV radiation of open space, returning alive and able to reproduce after 10 days in orbit.

children.name: Isohypsibius
children.description: A diverse eutardigrade genus found in freshwater mosses, lake sediments, and moist soil worldwide. Isohypsibius species tend to be associated with higher-moisture habitats than many Macrobiotus relatives, and several species are predominantly aquatic. The genus contains over 100 described species and continues to yield new records from poorly sampled tropical and polar regions.

children.name: Isohypsibius granulifer
children.description: Characterised by prominent granular ornamentation of its cuticle, I. granulifer inhabits mosses and algal mats in freshwater ponds and stream margins. It is one of the most easily identified Isohypsibius species in light microscopy due to its distinctive surface texture.

children.name: Isohypsibius sattleri
children.description: A freshwater-associated eutardigrade of mosses along stream banks and lake shores in temperate Europe and Asia. Described in the early twentieth century, it has been the subject of taxonomic revision as molecular methods clarify its boundaries from cryptic congeners.

children.name: Isohypsibius prosostomus
children.description: Distinguished by its projecting, anteriorly extended mouth cone, this species inhabits acidic boggy mosses and Sphagnum cushions in boreal and alpine zones. It feeds predominantly on algal cells and bacterioplankton films coating submerged plant surfaces.

children.name: Isohypsibius tuberculatus
children.description: A medium-sized isohypsibiid bearing small tubercles on its cuticle that are visible under high-magnification light microscopy. It occupies a broad range of moist microhabitats including Sphagnum bogs, mossy forest floors, and irrigated rock faces, and can survive short desiccation events.

children.name: Dactylobiotus
children.description: Large, conspicuous eutardigrades with distinctive elongated claws, Dactylobiotus species are primarily aquatic and semi-aquatic, inhabiting the sediments and mosses of ponds, lakes, and slow rivers. They are among the largest tardigrades, sometimes reaching 1 mm in length, and are voracious feeders on algae and detritus. Their size and aquatic lifestyle make them important components of freshwater meiofaunal communities.

children.name: Dactylobiotus macronyx
children.description: The largest commonly encountered eutardigrade, with notably elongated claws adapted for gripping aquatic vegetation and moss stems. It inhabits freshwater ponds and boggy margins, feeding on algae and plant cell contents, and is a reliable indicator of clean, oxygen-rich water.

children.name: Dactylobiotus dispar
children.description: Named for the unequal sizes of its claw pairs, D. dispar is an aquatic species found in lake sediment mosses and submerged algal mats. It is a useful model for studying aquatic tardigrade ecology and shows limited cryptobiotic ability compared to its terrestrial relatives.

children.name: Halobiotus
children.description: Marine intertidal eutardigrades remarkable for being among the very few tardigrades adapted to tolerate full salinity seawater. Halobiotus crispae from Roscoff, France is the best-studied, undergoing dramatic cyclomorphosis — seasonal changes in body form and physiology — as it alternates between active summer phases and dormant pseudosimplex stages that resist osmotic stress and winter cold. This genus bridges eutardigrade and marine tardigrade ecology.

children.name: Halobiotus crispae
children.description: The type species of Halobiotus and a subject of intensive physiological research for its seasonal cyclomorphosis. Found in intertidal rock pools and marine algal mats from France to Greenland, it exhibits a winter pseudosimplex stage that resists both osmotic shock and near-freezing temperatures.

children.name: Halobiotus stenostomus
children.description: A marine eutardigrade with a notably narrow buccal tube, found in intertidal and shallow subtidal algal mats in European coastal waters. Less studied than H. crispae, it appears to undergo similar seasonal dormancy and tolerates the salinity fluctuations typical of rocky intertidal pools.

children.name: Halobiotus florescens
children.description: Described from North Atlantic intertidal habitats, Halobiotus florescens is associated with encrusting coralline algae and rhodophyte mats in the lower intertidal zone. Its cyclomorphic biology parallels that of H. crispae, with seasonally distinct active and resistant forms.

children.name: Heterotardigrada
children.description: Distinguished by cephalic sensory appendages and, in many terrestrial species, a rigid cuticular armour divided into plates. Heterotardigrades include both marine arthrotardigrades and the strikingly coloured terrestrial echiniscids, which are often vivid red, orange, or yellow due to carotenoid pigments. They are found from deep-sea sediments to alpine snowfields.

children.name: Echiniscus
children.description: The most species-rich genus of heterotardigrades — squat, heavily armoured animals with a characteristic set of dorsal plates and lateral appendages. Their bright red or orange colour makes them visible under low-magnification microscopy as tiny jewels in a drop of moss water. Over 100 species are described, and new ones continue to be found in unexplored habitats from glacier forefields to tropical canopy.

children.name: Echiniscus wendti
children.description: A widely distributed armoured tardigrade with vivid red-orange pigment, common in mosses and lichens on rock and tree bark.

children.name: Echiniscus merokensis
children.description: A cosmopolitan echiniscid recorded from lowland mosses to high alpine and polar habitats, illustrating the broad environmental tolerance of armoured tardigrades.

children.name: Echiniscus canadensis
children.description: An armoured echiniscid first described from Canadian boreal mosses, where it inhabits cushions of Pleurozium and Dicranum on forest floors and boulders. Its bright orange-red colouration is typical of the genus and is due to carotenoid pigments of dietary origin.

children.name: Echiniscus spitsbergensis
children.description: A polar specialist described from Svalbard (Spitsbergen) Arctic mosses and reindeer moss lichens, and subsequently found in sub-Antarctic islands. It tolerates extended freezing, desiccation, and near-total darkness during polar winters by forming cryptobiotic tuns in its cuticular armour.

children.name: Echiniscus virginicus
children.description: First collected from mosses growing on limestone outcrops in Virginia, this echiniscid has subsequently been found across eastern North America and parts of South America. It is distinguished from congeners by the number and arrangement of its lateral appendages and the texture of its dorsal plates.

children.name: Echiniscus kofordi
children.description: Named after the American zoologist Charles Kofoid, this species inhabits epiphytic and epilithic mosses in temperate deciduous forests. It is recovered more often from humid north-facing exposures and is sensitive to prolonged drought relative to other echiniscids.

children.name: Echiniscus briophytarum
children.description: As its name implies, this echiniscid is intimately associated with bryophytes — liverworts as well as mosses — and rarely recovered from non-bryophyte substrates. It inhabits the moist microhabitats within thalloid liverwort mats and leafy moss cushions in temperate and boreal forests.

children.name: Echiniscus doryphorus
children.description: Named for its prominent spear-like lateral appendages, E. doryphorus inhabits cushion mosses and encrusting lichens on shaded rock faces in Europe and Asia. It is one of the more morphologically distinctive echiniscids and is readily identified by the elongate spine-like terminations of its posterior appendages.

children.name: Pseudechiniscus
children.description: Armoured heterotardigrades closely related to Echiniscus but lacking some of the lateral appendages characteristic of that genus. Pseudechiniscus species are found in mosses and lichens across the temperate world, and several have been described from Southern Hemisphere and oceanic island samples, suggesting either wide dispersal or ancient cosmopolitan distributions. Their taxonomy remains partially unresolved due to overlapping morphological characters.

children.name: Pseudechiniscus suillus
children.description: One of the first-described Pseudechiniscus species, found in mosses and lichens across temperate Europe, Asia, and the Americas. Its common name refers to the broad, rounded head region; it is identified by the reduced set of lateral appendages and the fine sculpturing of its dorsal plates.

children.name: Pseudechiniscus facettalis
children.description: Named for the facetted, polyhedral texture of its dorsal plate sculpture, this species is found in epilithic mosses and xerophytic lichens in warm-temperate and Mediterranean-climate zones. The facetted cuticle pattern is its most diagnostic feature and is clearly visible under scanning electron microscopy.

children.name: Pseudechiniscus juanitae
children.description: Described from Colombian and Venezuelan highland mosses, this Neotropical species inhabits the humid tepuis and montane bryophyte mats of northern South America. It is named in honour of a field collector who gathered the type material from cloud forest habitats.

children.name: Pseudechiniscus novaezeelandiae
children.description: An endemic heterotardigrade from New Zealand mosses and forest floor lichens, representing part of the distinctive Australasian tardigrade fauna with Gondwanan biogeographic affinities. It is found in both North and South Island temperate rainforest habitats and shows no known congeners in the immediate region.

children.name: Halechiniscus
children.description: Marine arthrotardigrades inhabiting the interstitial spaces between sand grains in shallow coastal sediments. Unlike their terrestrial relatives, arthrotardigrades rarely form cryptobiotic tuns — instead they tolerate the relatively stable conditions of marine sediment, moving through the sand using their stubby clawed legs in a slow, bear-like walk that earned the whole phylum its common name.

children.name: Halechiniscus greveni
children.description: A marine interstitial tardigrade living between sand grains in coastal sediments, moving with the slow clawed gait that gives water bears their name.

children.name: Batillipes
children.description: Marine interstitial arthrotardigrades with paddle-shaped feet adapted for gripping sand grains in their psammic habitat. Batillipes species are found in shallow marine sands from tropical beaches to polar coasts, and are among the most commonly encountered arthrotardigrades. They lack the cryptobiotic capabilities of their terrestrial relatives, relying instead on behavioural movement deeper into sediment to avoid surface desiccation and temperature extremes.

children.name: Batillipes mirus
children.description: The type species of Batillipes and one of the first marine tardigrades described, found in sublittoral and intertidal sands along European and North Atlantic coasts. Its paddle-shaped feet are adapted for gripping quartz grains in the interstitial habitat; it feeds on bacteria and unicellular algae coating sand surfaces.

children.name: Batillipes pennaki
children.description: Described from North American Atlantic coast sandy beaches, B. pennaki inhabits the upper layers of intertidal sand between the strandline and mean water level. It is named after the American meiofaunologist Robert Pennak, a pioneer of freshwater invertebrate zoology.

children.name: Batillipes tubernatis
children.description: An interstitial marine species characterised by small tubercles on its lateral body surface. It is found in medium-grained sublittoral sands at depths of one to ten metres and is widespread in warmer European and African coastal seas, representing part of the bathymetric range of Batillipes.

children.name: Batillipes noerrevangi
children.description: Described from Danish and North Sea intertidal sands, this species is named after the zoologist Arne Nørrevang. It inhabits the phreatic interstitial water films of coarse marine sediments and is tolerant of short-term hypoxia, a common condition in wave-disturbed beach sand.

children.name: Actinarctus
children.description: A small genus of marine arthrotardigrades with notably elaborate, star-like leg pads adapted for adhering to coarse sediment grains. Actinarctus species are found in shallow sublittoral and intertidal sandy habitats in European and North Atlantic waters. The genus is morphologically distinct from Batillipes in the radially arranged adhesive pads of its legs, a feature that has attracted interest in studies of biological adhesion.

children.name: Actinarctus doryphorus
children.description: The type and best-known species of Actinarctus, found in intertidal and shallow sublittoral coarse sands on European Atlantic and North Sea coasts. Its radially lobed, star-shaped adhesive pads are unique among arthrotardigrades and have been studied as examples of convergent biological adhesion systems.

children.name: Stygarctus
children.description: Deep-water and sublittoral marine arthrotardigrades, Stygarctus species are among the most phylogenetically basal heterotardigrades and are found in fine marine sediments from coastal muds to abyssal plains. They lack the highly modified adhesive feet of intertidal genera, instead possessing simple clawed legs suited to crawling through soft sediment. Their discovery in deep-sea samples extended the known range of tardigrades to extreme marine depths.

children.name: Stygarctus bradypus
children.description: The type species of Stygarctus, described from sublittoral muddy sediments in European coastal waters. Its name references its slow, deliberate locomotion through fine sediment. It has subsequently been found in North Atlantic and Pacific deep-sea samples, suggesting a broad bathymetric range.

children.name: Stygarctus estakesus
children.description: A sublittoral and bathyal Stygarctus collected from muddy and silty sediments in the northeastern Atlantic and Gulf of Mexico. It is distinguished from the type species by differences in its cephalic sensory appendage morphology and the proportions of its pharyngeal bulb.
